chunk_summary: This section explores the role of environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) in stunting and chronic undernutrition, focusing on its mechanisms, clinical implications, and diagnostic considerations.
learning_objectives: Understand the relationship between environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) and stunting in children.
Identify the clinical implications of EED in chronic undernutrition.
Learn why certain diagnostic tests may not be appropriate for EED.
key_facts: Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) is a subclinical condition linked to chronic undernutrition and stunting.
EED is characterized by intestinal inflammation, reduced nutrient absorption, and altered gut microbiota.
Stunting affects approximately 22% of children under 5 globally, with EED being a significant contributing factor.
EED is often associated with poor sanitation and repeated exposure to enteric pathogens.
There is no single diagnostic test for EED; clinical management focuses on improving nutrition and sanitation.
reference_quote: EED is a subclinical condition that contributes significantly to stunting and chronic undernutrition, particularly in low-resource settings.
